title: Modern Kubernetes 1.36: Workloads, Security, Networking, and Operations
description: Developers and operators who understand Linux and Docker and need to design, deploy, secure, debug, package, scale, upgrade, and defend production-shaped Kubernetes applications. This original, research-backed path uses repeated retrieval, cumulative artifacts, changed-case labs, stakeholder projects, and a performance exam.
outcomes: Demonstrate kubernetes 1.36.2 architecture, api discovery, reconciliation, kubectl context safety, metadata, namespaces, pods, configuration, and observable object-state evidence through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate workload controllers, probes, resources, services, endpointslices, dns, networkpolicy, gateway api 1.5.1, persistent storage, scheduling, disruption, and autoscaling through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate service accounts, least-privilege rbac, secrets, pod security, workload isolation, admission, multi-tenancy, observability, debugging, and incident reconstruction through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate kustomize through kubectl 1.36, helm 4.2.2, extensions, operators, minikube 1.38.1, immutable promotion, conformance, upgrades, fault testing, disaster recovery, and production transfer through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

competencies.statement: Trace API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager, kubelet, container runtime, network proxy, DNS, and add-on responsibilities for one workload transition.
misconceptions: The scheduler starts containers and continuously repairs every unhealthy application.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Trace API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager, kubelet, container runtime, network proxy, DNS, and add-on responsibilities for one workload transition.

competencies.statement: Explain how API requests, admission, persisted desired state, watches, caches, and component status relate without treating any one cache as authority.
misconceptions: Reading etcd directly is the normal and safest way for applications to discover Kubernetes state.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Explain how API requests, admission, persisted desired state, watches, caches, and component status relate without treating any one cache as authority.

competencies.statement: Trace desired state, observed state, controller decisions, idempotent actions, status updates, and eventual convergence through a changed failure.
misconceptions: Applying a manifest runs its YAML instructions once in top-to-bottom order.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Trace desired state, observed state, controller decisions, idempotent actions, status updates, and eventual convergence through a changed failure.

masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Distinguish Kubernetes core, distribution, cloud provider, CNI, CSI, CRI, Gateway implementation, add-on, and application responsibilities.

misconceptions: kubectl version alone proves the current context, server compatibility, and enabled APIs.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Capture client, server, API discovery, context, cluster identity, node, runtime, add-on, feature-state, and support-window evidence before mutation.

misconceptions: Creating a ServiceAccount creates a safe long-lived Secret token for external automation.

competencies.statement: Protect Secret access with least RBAC, encryption at rest, key rotation, audit, node and backup controls, external identity, redaction, and incident response.
